metadata.title: Build a Pricing Strategy Analyzer with Claude Code

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Pricing methodology depends entirely on what kind of product you are pricing. A new invention, a modification, a competitive commodity — each has a different dominant methodology, and the wrong choice produces the wrong questions. Most candidates apply value-based pricing to everything. What does a product-category-aware pricing analysis look like?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- title card: PRICING STRATEGY ANALYZER; the problem: one-size pricing vs. category-aware methodology

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Pricing methodology depends entirely on what kind of product you are pricing. A new invention, a modification, a competitive commodity — each has a different do

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to build a Python CLI that classifies a product description into one of four categories, selects the dominant pricing methodology for that category, and outputs the three questions that must be answered before setting price — each with a specific worked example.

props.command:
claude "Build a Python CLI pricing analyzer.
Input: a product description.
Output: (1) product category (new invention,
modification of existing, similar-to-existing,
portfolio pricing), with one-sentence justification;
(2) dominant pricing methodology for this category;
(3) the three most important questions that must
be answered before setting price, each with a
specific example of how to answer it for this product.
Use the anthropic Python SDK."

beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The prompt engineering is the key part: the script explicitly names the four categories and the four methodologies so Claude cannot slip into a generic analysis. Verify the classification prompt is sensitive to the product description — a revolutionary product should route to new invention, not similar-to-existing.

props.code:
# pricing_analyzer.py -- Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

CATEGORIES = [
    "new invention (no comparable product exists)",
    "modification of existing (same base, improved)",
    "similar-to-existing (competitive commodity)",
    "portfolio pricing (bundle or family effect)",
]
METHODS = [
    "value-based (willingness to pay)",
    "cost-plus (floor + margin)",
    "competitor-based (parity or premium/discount)",
    "portfolio (internal cannibalization logic)",
]

def analyze(product):
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Product: {product}\n\n"
            f"Categories: {CATEGORIES}\n"
            f"Methods: {METHODS}\n\n"
            "Output category, method, 3 questions w/ examples."
        }]
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

product = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or \
    "AI-powered MRI contrast agent for rare cancers"
print(analyze(product))

beats.narration_text: Run it on a genuinely novel medical device — an AI-powered MRI contrast agent for rare cancers. Category: new invention. Methodology: value-based, because no comparable product defines a market price floor. The three questions: what does a diagnosis six months earlier save in treatment cost? What does the radiologist's time saved translate to in billing? What is the reimbursement ceiling the insurer will approve?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording of terminal: new invention classification; value-based methodology; three value questions with examples

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Run it on a genuinely novel medical device — an AI-powered MRI contrast agent for rare cancers. Category: new invention. Methodology: value-based, because no co

beats.narration_text: Now run the sanity check. Ask Claude to surface the most common mistake analysts make with this methodology — and check whether this product description already shows signs of that mistake.

props.command:
claude "Modify pricing_analyzer.py:
After the three questions, add a sanity check:
'For the methodology selected, what is the most
common mistake analysts make -- and does this
product description show any signs of that mistake?'
Display the meta-level insight below the questions."

beats.narration_text: The sanity check appears. Most common mistake with value-based pricing: anchoring willingness to pay on cost of production rather than value delivered to the buyer. Does this product description show signs of it? Yes — the description mentions manufacturing complexity, which is irrelevant to the buyer's willingness to pay. Strip the cost framing; restate the analysis in terms of clinical value delivered.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: three questions plus sanity check section; mistake identified; fix stated

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The sanity check appears. Most common mistake with value-based pricing: anchoring willingness to pay on cost of production rather than value delivered to the bu

beats.narration_text: Price is not a function of cost. It is the mechanism by which a seller captures a fraction of the value a buyer receives. Getting that fraction right requires knowing what the buyer values — and cost, which is entirely about the seller, tells you almost nothing about that.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: price as buyer-value fraction; cost is irrelevant to WTP

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Price is not a function of cost. It is the mechanism by which a seller captures a fraction of the value a buyer receives. Getting that fraction right requires k

beats.narration_text: Run two contrasting products: the novel medical device and a tenth-generation consumer smartphone. The methodology should differ — value-based versus competitor-based — and the questions should differ accordingly. If the questions look similar across both, the category classification is not doing its job.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps card: run novel vs. commodity; check question divergence by category

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Run two contrasting products: the novel medical device and a tenth-generation consumer smartphone. The methodology should differ — value-based versus competitor

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
